title: The Social Contract
author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
original_title: Du contrat social; ou, Principes du droit politique
author_bio: Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778) was a Genevan philosopher, writer, and composer. Born in the Republic of Geneva, he spent most of his adult life in France. His political philosophy influenced the Age of Enlightenment and the French Revolution. Notable works include The Social Contract, Émile, Discourse on Inequality, and Confessions.
influenced: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen

related_books.title: Leviathan
related_books.author: Thomas Hobbes
related_books.relationship: Hobbes's social contract theory serves as the foil Rousseau argues against

related_books.title: Two Treatises of Government
related_books.author: John Locke
related_books.relationship: Locke's consent-based government influenced Rousseau, who extended the argument

related_books.title: Democracy in America
related_books.author: Alexis de Tocqueville
related_books.relationship: Tocqueville's study of American democracy addresses Rousseauian questions about civic participation

related_books.title: The Spirit of the Laws
related_books.author: Montesquieu
related_books.relationship: Montesquieu's analysis of government types informed Rousseau's classification

related_books.title: Discourse on Inequality
related_books.author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
related_books.relationship: Rousseau's earlier work tracing how humanity lost its natural freedom

controversy: Banned and burned in Paris and Geneva in June 1762; Rousseau fled to Prussia. The controversy centered on the chapter on civil religion.
posthumous_honor: Remains moved to the Pantheon in Paris in 1794 by the French Revolutionary government
epigraph: Foederis aequas dicamus leges (Virgil, Aeneid XI.321-22)
recommended_for: Citizens concerned with legitimacy and consent
